1	---
2	name: blueprint-clean
3	description: Post-write blueprint gate. Strips Lean leakage, project history, and verbosity. Validates and inserts missing source quotes from PDFs/TeXs.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch this subagent AFTER a blueprint-writer round and BEFORE dispatching provers.
10	  - Required to ensure blueprint purity (math-only).
11	  - Include `references/**` in its write-domain so it can spawn `reference-retriever` if needed.
12	---
13	
14	# Blueprint Clean Subagent
15	
16	Your job is to read and refine a blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) to ensure it meets strict quality, purity, and citation standards.
17	
18	## Your Tasks
19	
20	1. **Strip Lean Leakage & Project History:**
21	   - Remove any mention of Lean tactics, typeclass notes, or specific Lean implementation strategies.
22	   - Remove project history references like "since iteration N", "our failed route", etc.
23	   - Ensure the blueprint reads as a timeless, standalone mathematical document.
24	2. **Trim Verbosity:**
25	   - Remove useless, overly conversational paragraphs.
26	   - Remove passages that are irrelevant or redundantly discuss things already implemented, unless they serve a mathematical purpose.
27	   - Keep the prose concise and mathematically precise.
28	3. **Enforce Citation Discipline & Add Quotes:**
29	   - Verify every block derived from a reference has a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`.
30	   - **If the quote is missing** but the reference exists in `references/`, you MUST open the actual reference `.pdf` or `.tex` file (using the `.md` index file as a pointer to find the section/page), extract the verbatim quote, and add it to the blueprint.
31	   - **If a reference SHOULD be cited** but isn't mentioned by the writer, and the source is missing from `references/`, spawn a `reference-retriever` to fetch it.
32	4. **Fix LaTeX:**
33	   - Correct basic LaTeX syntax errors.
34	   - Ensure `\uses{}` and `\label{}` cross-references are formatted correctly.
35	
36	## Spawning a Retriever
37	If you identify a missing reference, spawn it using:
38	```bash
39	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name reference-retriever --slug <slug> --directive-file <file> --write-domain 'references/**'
40	```
41	
42	Return your outcome and the path to your report.
43

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}
83	
84	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
85	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
86	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
87	  \mathlibok
88	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
89	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
90	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
91	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
92	  \[
93	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
94	  \]
95	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
96	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
97	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
98	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
99	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
100	\end{lemma}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
103	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
104	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
107	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
108	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
109	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
110	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
111	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
112	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
113	  the section graded ring.
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	\begin{definition}\leanok
117	[Presheaf-of-modules category in monoidal form]
118	  \label{def:monoidalPresheaf}
119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.MonoidalPresheaf}
120	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
121	  For a scheme \(X\), write \(\PshMod_{\otimes}(X)\) for the category of presheaves
122	  of modules over the underlying presheaf of (plain) rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\),
123	  obtained by composing the structure sheaf with the forgetful functor
124	  \(\mathrm{CommRingCat} \to \mathrm{RingCat}\). This is definitionally
125	  \(X.\PshMod\), but presented in the exact form for which Mathlib equips it with
126	  the symmetric monoidal structure of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib};
127	  it is the carrier on which the objectwise tensor product of
128	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} is taken.
129	\end{definition}
130	
131	\begin{definition}\leanok
132	[Scheme-level module sheafification functor]
133	  \label{def:schemeModuleSheafification}
134	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafification}
135	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
136	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{module sheafification functor}
137	  \((-)^{\#} : X.\PshMod \to X.\Modules\) is Mathlib's sheafification functor
138	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) specialised to the identity
139	  morphism of the underlying presheaf of rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (a locally
140	  bijective map). Applying it to the objectwise tensor presheaf is what defines the
141	  sheaf tensor product \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}.
142	\end{definition}
143	
144	\begin{definition}\leanok
145	[Unit module of a scheme]
146	  \label{def:unitModule}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitModule}
148	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
149	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{unit module} \(\mathbf{1}_X \in X.\Modules\) is the
150	  structure sheaf viewed as a module over itself, i.e.\ Mathlib's
151	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.unit}\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) for
152	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It is the value of the zeroth tensor power,
153	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}).
154	\end{definition}
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
158	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
160	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
161	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
162	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
163	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
164	  % L2282-L2297).
165	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
166	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
167	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
168	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
169	  %  $$
170	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
171	  %  $$
172	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
173	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
174	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
175	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
176	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
177	  %  $$
178	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
179	  %  =
180	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
181	  %  $$"
182	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
183	  product).}
184	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
185	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
186	  \[
187	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
188	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
189	  \]
190	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
191	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
192	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
193	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
194	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
195	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
196	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
197	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
198	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
199	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
200	\end{definition}
201	
202	\begin{definition}\leanok
203	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
204	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
206	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
207	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
208	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
209	  % L4220-L4228).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
212	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
213	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
214	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
215	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
216	  %  exactly $n$ times."
217	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
218	  powers).}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
220	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
221	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
222	  on \(m\):
223	  \[
224	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
225	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
226	    \qquad
227	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
228	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
229	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
230	  \]
231	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
232	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
233	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
234	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
235	  invertible.
236	\end{definition}
237	
238	\begin{lemma}\leanok
239	[Zeroth tensor power is the unit]
240	  \label{lem:tensorPow_zero}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_zero}
242	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:unitModule}
243	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
244	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\), the unit module
245	  (\cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of the recursive definition
246	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
247	\end{lemma}
248	
249	\begin{lemma}\leanok
250	[Tensor-power recursion step]
251	  \label{lem:tensorPow_succ}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_succ}
253	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
254	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) and \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) one has
255	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
256	  \mathcal{L}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); this is the successor clause of
257	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{definition}\leanok
261	[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
262	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
264	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
265	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
266	  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
267	  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
268	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
269	  \[
270	    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
271	      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
272	      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
273	  \]
274	  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
275	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
276	  \mathcal{F}\).
277	\end{definition}
278	
279	\begin{lemma}\leanok
280	[Zeroth twist]
281	  \label{lem:moduleTensorPow_zero}
282	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow_zero}
283	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule,
284	    def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
285	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
286	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X\)
287	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of
288	  \cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}, holding by definition, and is canonically
289	  isomorphic to \(\mathcal{F}\) via the right unitor
290	  (\cref{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}).
291	\end{lemma}
292	
293	The next four isomorphisms are the parts of the (would-be) symmetric monoidal
294	structure on \(X.\Modules\) that descend through sheafification from
295	\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} using only \emph{functoriality} of the
296	sheafification functor (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) and, for the
297	unitors, the reflective counit isomorphism --- no strong monoidality of
298	sheafification is required. They are the structural inputs of the tensor-power
299	comparison \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
300	
301	\begin{definition}\leanok
302	[Counit isomorphism of module sheafification]
303	  \label{def:sheafificationCounitIso}
304	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafificationCounitIso}
305	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
306	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) the counit of the sheafification
307	  adjunction (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
308	  \cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) gives a canonical isomorphism
309	  \((\mathcal{G})^{\#} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) of sheaves of modules,
310	  invertible because the right adjoint forgetful functor to presheaves of modules
311	  is fully faithful.
312	\end{definition}
313	
314	\begin{definition}\leanok
315	[Left unitor of the sheaf tensor product]
316	  \label{def:tensorObjUnitIso}
317	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjUnitIso}
318	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
319	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
320	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical left-unitor
321	  isomorphism \(\mathbf{1}_X \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
322	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
323	  \cref{def:unitModule}), obtained by sheafifying the presheaf left unitor of
324	  \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} and composing with the counit
325	  isomorphism \cref{def:sheafificationCounitIso}. It is the base case
326	  (\(m = 0\)) of \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
327	\end{definition}
328	
329	\begin{definition}\leanok
330	[Right unitor of the sheaf tensor product]
331	  \label{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjRightUnitor}
333	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
334	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
335	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical right-unitor
336	  isomorphism \(\mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X
337	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
338	  \cref{def:unitModule}), the sheafification of the presheaf right unitor
339	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) composed with the counit
340	  isomorphism \cref{def:sheafificationCounitIso}.
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\begin{definition}\leanok
344	[Braiding of the sheaf tensor product]
345	  \label{def:tensorBraiding}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorBraiding}
347	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:schemeModuleSheafification,
348	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
349	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical
350	  braiding isomorphism \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
351	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}\)
352	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), obtained by sheafifying
353	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) the symmetric braiding of the presheaf
354	  monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). It is the
355	  symmetry used in the inductive step of \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
356	\end{definition}
357	
358	The strong-monoidality comparison \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
359	below is assembled from three reduction lemmas --- a localization criterion, the
360	local-isomorphism property of the unit, and their corollary --- together with the
361	coequalizer presentation of the relative module-presheaf tensor product, the single
362	Mathlib-absent brick on which the comparison rests.
363	
364	\begin{lemma}\leanok
365	[Localization criterion for module sheafification]
366	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
367	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
368	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
369	  For a morphism \(f : \mathcal{P} \to \mathcal{Q}\) of presheaves of
370	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the sheafification \((f)^{\#}\)
371	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules if
372	  and only if the underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves lies in the
373	  weak-equivalence class \(J.W\) of the opens topology \(J\) on \(X\) (i.e.\ is a
374	  local isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves).
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
379	  Module sheafification (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is the localization
380	  functor at the class \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying
381	  abelian-presheaf morphism lies in \(J.W\), i.e.\ the inverse image of \(J.W\) along
382	  the forgetful functor. The localization criterion for a reflective adjunction
383	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) then gives the stated
384	  equivalence.
385	\end{proof}
386	
387	\begin{lemma}\leanok
388	[The sheafification unit is an abelian local isomorphism]
389	  \label{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
390	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
391	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
392	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the underlying
393	  abelian-group presheaf morphism of the sheafification unit
394	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\)
395	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) is the abelian sheafification
396	  unit \(\mathcal{P} \to a(\mathcal{P})\); it therefore lies in the weak-equivalence
397	  class \(J.W\) of the opens topology.
398	\end{lemma}
399	
400	\begin{proof}
401	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
402	  The forgetful functor to abelian-group presheaves carries the module
403	  sheafification unit to the unit of the abelian associated-sheaf adjunction, which
404	  is a local isomorphism, hence a member of \(J.W\).
405	\end{proof}
406	
407	\begin{lemma}\leanok
408	[Sheafification inverts the localization unit]
409	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
410	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
411	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
412	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the sheafification
413	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})^{\#}\) of the localization unit is an isomorphism of sheaves
414	  of modules. This is the un-whiskered (\(m = 0\)) special case of
415	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}.
416	\end{lemma}
417	
418	\begin{proof}
419	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
420	  Feed the abelian local-isomorphism statement
421	  \cref{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit} through the localization criterion
422	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}.
423	\end{proof}
424	
425	\begin{definition}\leanok
426	[Step-1 objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf]
427	  \label{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
428	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorDomainPresheaf}
429	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
430	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
431	  assignment \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\),
432	  with restriction maps the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of the underlying restriction
433	  maps, is a functor \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\). It is
434	  the codomain row of the relative-tensor coequalizer presentation
435	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}); functoriality follows from the
436	  functoriality of \(\mathrm{TensorProduct.map}\) (identity and composition laws
437	  by \(\otimes\)-induction).
438	\end{definition}
439	
440	\begin{definition}\leanok
441	[Step-1 objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor triple presheaf]
442	  \label{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}
443	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorTriplePresheaf}
444	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
445	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
446	  assignment
447	  \[
448	    U \;\longmapsto\;
449	      \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
450	        \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr),
451	  \]
452	  with restriction maps the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of the three underlying
453	  restriction maps --- those of \(\mathcal{P}\), of the structure presheaf
454	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (the middle factor restricting along the ring restriction
455	  map), and of \(\mathcal{Q}\) --- is a functor
456	  \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\). It is the \emph{domain}
457	  row of the relative-tensor coequalizer presentation
458	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}): objectwise it is exactly the
459	  triple tensor \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
460	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) on which the two
461	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps of
462	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} act, its codomain row being
463	  the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf \cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}.
464	  Functoriality (the identity and composition laws of the restriction maps)
465	  follows from the functoriality of \(\mathrm{TensorProduct.map}\) --- the
466	  \(\mathrm{map\_id}\) and \(\mathrm{map\_comp}\) laws --- checked on elementary
467	  tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
468	\end{definition}
469	
470	\begin{definition}\leanok
471	[Left-action natural transformation of the coequalizer]
472	  \label{def:relTensorActL}
473	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorActL}
474	  \uses{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
475	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
476	  \emph{left-action} natural transformation
477	  \[
478	    a_L : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
479	      (\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U))\bigr)
480	    \;\longrightarrow\;
481	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
482	  \]
483	  from the triple presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the
484	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) is, on an
485	  open \(U\), the additive map
486	  \[
487	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
488	      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
489	    \longrightarrow
490	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
491	    \qquad
492	    m \otimes (s \otimes n) \longmapsto (s \cdot m) \otimes n,
493	  \]
494	  collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar action of
495	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) on \(\mathcal{P}(U)\). This is exactly the objectwise
496	  action map \(\mathrm{actLmap}\) of
497	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}; together with the parallel
498	  right-action transformation it is one of the two rows whose coequalizer
499	  presents the relative tensor presheaf \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
500	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}). Naturality in \(U\) is the
501	  compatibility of the module action with the restriction maps: the restriction
502	  of \(s \cdot m\) equals \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), so \(a_L\) commutes with the
503	  triple-tensor and \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor restriction maps; this is checked on
504	  elementary tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
505	\end{definition}
506	
507	\begin{definition}\leanok
508	[Right-action natural transformation of the coequalizer]
509	  \label{def:relTensorActR}
510	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorActR}
511	  \uses{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
512	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
513	  \emph{right-action} natural transformation
514	  \[
515	    a_R : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
516	      (\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U))\bigr)
517	    \;\longrightarrow\;
518	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
519	  \]
520	  from the triple presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the
521	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) is, on an
522	  open \(U\), the additive map
523	  \[
524	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
525	      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
526	    \longrightarrow
527	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
528	    \qquad
529	    m \otimes (s \otimes n) \longmapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n),
530	  \]
531	  collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar action of
532	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) on \(\mathcal{Q}(U)\). This is exactly the objectwise
533	  action map \(\mathrm{actRmap}\) of
534	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}, the mirror of the left-action
535	  transformation \(a_L\) (\cref{def:relTensorActL}); together \(a_L\) and \(a_R\)
536	  form the parallel pair whose coequalizer presents the relative tensor presheaf
537	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\). Naturality in \(U\) is the compatibility
538	  of the module action with the restriction maps: the restriction of
539	  \(s \cdot n\) equals \((s|_V) \cdot (n|_V)\), so \(a_R\) commutes with the
540	  triple-tensor and \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor restriction maps. The verification
541	  reduces to the single fact that the restriction maps of \(\mathcal{Q}\) are
542	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-semilinear (\(\mathrm{PresheafOfModules.map\_smul}\) applied
543	  to \(\mathcal{Q}\)), checked on elementary tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
544	\end{definition}
545	
546	\begin{definition}
547	[Projection natural transformation of the coequalizer]
548	  \label{def:relTensorProj}
549	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorProj}
550	  \uses{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
551	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
552	  \emph{projection} natural transformation
553	  \[
554	    \pi : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
555	      \;\longrightarrow\;
556	      \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
557	  \]
558	  from the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) to
559	  the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the presheaf-level monoidal tensor
560	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\) (Mathlib's
561	  \(\mathrm{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\)) is, on an open \(U\), the
562	  canonical quotient additive map
563	  \[
564	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
565	    \longrightarrow
566	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U),
567	    \qquad
568	    m \otimes n \longmapsto m \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} n,
569	  \]
570	  the objectwise projection \(\mathrm{projL}\) of
571	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}. It is the cofork of the
572	  relative-tensor coequalizer: the third leg, the cokernel of \(a_L - a_R\)
573	  (\cref{def:relTensorActL}, \cref{def:relTensorActR}). The apex is identified
574	  with the objectwise value of the presheaf monoidal product through
575	  \cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}.
576	
577	  \emph{Naturality.} The objectwise square asserts
578	  \[
579	    \pi_V \circ
580	      \bigl(\mathrm{restr}_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
581	        \mathrm{restr}_{\mathcal{Q}}\bigr)
582	    \;=\;
583	    \bigl((\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})(f)\bigr) \circ \pi_U
584	    \qquad (f : V \to U),
585	  \]
586	  both composites sending \(m \otimes n\) to
587	  \(\bigl(m|_V\bigr) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(V)} \bigl(n|_V\bigr)\). On elementary
588	  tensors the left-hand side reduces by functoriality of the
589	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of restriction maps followed by the defining formula of
590	  \(\mathrm{projL}\); the right-hand side reduces by \(\mathrm{projL}\) followed
591	  by the objectwise restriction formula for the presheaf monoidal product
592	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}, on elementary tensors:
593	  \((\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})(f)\,(m \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} n) =
594	  (m|_V) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(V)} (n|_V)\)). The two sides then agree by
595	  \(\otimes\)-induction.
596	
597	  \emph{Route for the naturality verification.} The obstruction is purely one of
598	  identifying two presentations of the apex restriction. The restriction used on
599	  the right-hand side above is the \emph{module}-presheaf restriction
600	  \((\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})(f)\), for which the elementary-tensor
601	  formula is directly available; but the codomain of \(\pi\) as written is the
602	  \emph{abelian}-group presheaf underlying \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
603	  whose restriction is the forgetful image of the module restriction. Reconciling
604	  the two re-elaborates \(\pi\) against the abelian codomain and forces the
605	  base ring of the relative tensor to be read through the forgetful functor
606	  \(\mathrm{CommRingCat} \to \mathrm{RingCat}\) (the carrier
607	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) viewed as a plain ring), which is the same ring as the
608	  \(\mathrm{CommRingCat}\)-carrier \(\pi\) is built over but presented
609	  differently. The chosen route is to prove the naturality square \emph{at the
610	  level of presheaves of modules} --- exhibiting \(\pi\) as the image under the
611	  forgetful functor \(\PshMod \to (\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to
612	  \mathrm{Ab}\) of a morphism of \emph{module} presheaves, where the
613	  elementary-tensor restriction formula of
614	  \cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib} applies on the nose --- and only then
615	  forget to abelian groups. Equivalently, a single carrier-transport lemma
616	  identifying the \(\mathrm{RingCat}\)-carrier and \(\mathrm{CommRingCat}\)-carrier
617	  module structures on \(\mathcal{P}(V)\) lets one rewrite the apex restriction
618	  into module-presheaf form before applying the projection formula. The
619	  module-presheaf-level morphism, naturality proved there, is the concrete
620	  intermediate to construct; the abelian \(\pi\) is its forgetful image.
621	\end{definition}
622	
623	\begin{lemma}[A \(\mathbb{Z}\)-whiskered stalkwise isomorphism is a local isomorphism]
624	  \label{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}
625	  % NOTE: Lean decl name pending --- no `\lean{}` pin yet; add when the Lean
626	  % declaration for this whiskered-stalkwise-iso lemma lands.
627	  \uses{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
628	  Let \(f : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be a morphism of abelian-group
629	  presheaves on the space \(X\) that is a \emph{stalkwise isomorphism} (each
630	  stalk map \(f_x\) is an isomorphism), and let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a further
631	  abelian-group presheaf. Then the whiskered map
632	  \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{C}}\) of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor
633	  presheaves is again a stalkwise isomorphism, hence a local isomorphism (a
634	  member of \(J.W\) for the opens topology \(J\)).
635	\end{lemma}
636	
637	\begin{proof}
638	  \uses{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
639	  The stalk functor \(\mathcal{A} \mapsto \mathcal{A}_x\) at a point \(x\) is a
640	  filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods of \(x\), and \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\)
641	  commutes with filtered colimits, so
642	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{C})_x \cong
643	  \mathcal{A}_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{C}_x\) naturally in \(\mathcal{A}\).
644	  Under this identification the stalk of \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) is
645	  \(f_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), an isomorphism since \(f_x\) is.
646	  Thus \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) is a stalkwise isomorphism; a
647	  stalkwise isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves on a space is exactly a local
648	  isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\).
649	\end{proof}

651	\begin{lemma}[Abelian universal property of the relative tensor product]
652	  \label{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
653	  \lean{TensorProduct.liftAddHom}
654	  \mathlibok
655	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.Basic}).}
656	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N, P\) be \(S\)-modules (or, more
657	  generally, the abelian-group statement: \(M, N\) modules and \(P\) an additive
658	  commutative group). An additive-bilinear map
659	  \(f : M \to (N \to P)\) that is \emph{\(S\)-balanced}, i.e.\ satisfies
660	  \(f(s \cdot m)(n) = f(m)(s \cdot n)\) for all \(s \in S\), factors uniquely
661	  through the relative tensor product as an additive group homomorphism
662	  \(\widetilde{f} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with
663	  \(\widetilde{f}(m \otimes_S n) = f(m)(n)\). This is the abelian-group universal
664	  property of \(M \otimes_S N\): it is the quotient of \(M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N\)
665	  by the \(S\)-balancing relations.
666	\end{lemma}
667	
668	\begin{lemma}[The relative tensor of modules is a coequalizer of abelian groups]\leanok
669	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
670	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork,
671	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actN,
672	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actM,
673	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap,
674	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap,
675	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL,
676	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_surjective,
677	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_comp_act,
678	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aL,
679	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aR,
680	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor,
681	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_epi,
682	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.coeq_condition,
683	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.cofork,
684	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom,
685	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descMor,
686	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descFac,
687	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap_tmul,
688	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap_tmul,
689	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_tmul,
690	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_apply,
691	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom_tmul}
692	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
693	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N\) be \(S\)-modules. The abelian group
694	  \(M \otimes_S N\) is the coequalizer, \emph{in the category
695	  \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of abelian groups}, of the two parallel
696	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear \(S\)-action maps
697	  \[
698	    a_L,\, a_R :
699	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N)
700	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
701	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N,
702	  \]
703	  where the \emph{left} action map sends \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto
704	  (s \cdot m) \otimes n\) and the \emph{right} action map sends
705	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n)\). The coforking map is
706	  the canonical projection \(\pi : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to M \otimes_S N\),
707	  \(m \otimes n \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
708	\end{lemma}
709	
710	\begin{proof}
711	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
712	  Write \(a_N : S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to N\) and
713	  \(a_M : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} S \to M\) for the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear maps
714	  induced by the scalar action (\(s \otimes n \mapsto s \cdot n\),
715	  \(m \otimes s \mapsto s \cdot m\)); the right action map is
716	  \(a_R = \mathrm{id}_M \otimes a_N\) and the left action map is
717	  \(a_L = (a_M \otimes \mathrm{id}_N) \circ \mathrm{assoc}^{-1}\), where
718	  \(\mathrm{assoc}\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor associator. The projection
719	  \(\pi\) is the additive homomorphism produced by the abelian universal property
720	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} from the \(S\)-balanced bilinear map
721	  \((m, n) \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
722	
723	  \emph{Cofork condition.} On an elementary tensor
724	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n)\) we have
725	  \(\pi(a_L(m \otimes s \otimes n)) = (s \cdot m) \otimes_S n
726	  = m \otimes_S (s \cdot n) = \pi(a_R(m \otimes s \otimes n))\), the middle
727	  equality being the defining \(S\)-balancing relation of \(M \otimes_S N\); since
728	  elementary tensors generate, \(\pi \circ a_L = \pi \circ a_R\).
729	
730	  \emph{Universal property.} Given any abelian group \(P\) and a homomorphism
731	  \(g : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to P\) with \(g \circ a_L = g \circ a_R\), the
732	  bilinear map \((m, n) \mapsto g(m \otimes n)\) is \(S\)-balanced (that is exactly
733	  the cofork condition read on elementary tensors), so by
734	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} it factors as a unique
735	  \(\widetilde{g} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi = g\).
736	  Existence is this descended map; uniqueness follows because \(\pi\) is
737	  surjective, hence an epimorphism of abelian groups, so \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi
738	  = g\) determines \(\widetilde{g}\). Packaging existence and uniqueness gives the
739	  colimit cofork.
740	
741	\end{proof}

743	\begin{lemma}[Relative tensor of module presheaves as a coequalizer]
744	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}
746	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
747	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
748	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
749	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib,
750	    def:relTensorActL, def:relTensorActR, def:relTensorProj}
751	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
752	  underlying abelian-group presheaf of their relative tensor product
753	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
754	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is naturally the coequalizer of the
755	  parallel pair
756	  \[
757	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
758	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
759	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
760	  \]
761	  of abelian-group presheaves, where the first map acts the ring factor on the left,
762	  \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto (r \cdot p) \otimes q\), and the second on the
763	  right, \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto p \otimes (r \cdot q)\). The identification
764	  is objectwise and natural in \(U\).
765	\end{lemma}
766	
767	\begin{proof}
768	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
769	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
770	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
771	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib,
772	    def:relTensorActL, def:relTensorActR, def:relTensorProj}
773	  The argument promotes the objectwise coequalizer of
774	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} to the functor category, in
775	  three steps.
776	
777	  \textbf{1. Objectwise.} For each open \(U\), apply
778	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} with \(S = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
779	  \(M = \mathcal{P}(U)\), \(N = \mathcal{Q}(U)\): the abelian group
780	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is the coequalizer
781	  in \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps
782	  \[
783	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
784	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
785	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
786	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
787	  \]
788	  with cofork map the projection \(\pi_U\).
789	
790	  \textbf{2. Naturality / promotion.} The three objectwise families are precisely
791	  the natural transformations of presheaves on \(X\)
792	  \[
793	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
794	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
795	    \;\overset{a_L,\,a_R}{\rightrightarrows}\;
796	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
797	    \;\overset{\pi}{\longrightarrow}\;
798	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
799	  \]
800	  built in the previous definitions: the parallel pair is the left- and
801	  right-action transformations \(a_L\) (\cref{def:relTensorActL}) and \(a_R\)
802	  (\cref{def:relTensorActR}) from the triple presheaf
803	  (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf
804	  (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}), and the cofork leg is the projection
805	  \(\pi\) (\cref{def:relTensorProj}) onto the apex. These are morphisms of
806	  functors \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\); naturality of
807	  each in \(U\) is the compatibility of the module action with the restriction
808	  maps of \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\) (the restriction of
809	  \(s \cdot m\) is \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), and likewise for the projection),
810	  recorded in those definitions. Objectwise at each \(U\) the cofork
811	  \((a_L, a_R, \pi)_U\) is the colimit cofork of step 1. Colimits in a functor
812	  category are computed objectwise --- by
813	  \cref{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib} a cocone is a colimit as
814	  soon as every evaluation \((\mathrm{ev}_U)\) of it is --- so the objectwise
815	  coequalizers of step 1 promote to a coequalizer of the displayed parallel pair
816	  \(a_L, a_R\) with cofork \(\pi\) in
817	  \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\).
818	
819	  \textbf{3. Identification of the apex.} The apex presheaf
820	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the
821	  objectwise tensor formula of the presheaf monoidal structure
822	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}: \((\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes_p
823	  \mathcal{M}_2)(U) = \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
824	  \mathcal{M}_2(U)\)), exactly the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the
825	  relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
826	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Transporting the colimit of step 2
827	  along this identification exhibits
828	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\), as an abelian-group
829	  presheaf, as the coequalizer of the parallel pair, naturally in \(U\).
830	\end{proof}
831	
832	\begin{lemma}[Objectwise colimits in a functor category]
833	  \label{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib}
834	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Limits.evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits}
835	  \mathlibok
836	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
837	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.FunctorCategory.Basic}).}
838	  Let \(\mathcal{D}\) be a category and \(\mathcal{A}\) a category in which the
839	  relevant colimits exist. A cocone over a diagram
840	  \(F : \mathcal{J} \to (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A})\) in the functor category is
841	  a colimit cocone as soon as, for every object \(U\) of \(\mathcal{D}\), its image
842	  under evaluation \(\mathrm{ev}_U : (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A}) \to \mathcal{A}\)
843	  is a colimit cocone in \(\mathcal{A}\). Equivalently, colimits in a functor
844	  category are computed objectwise.
845	\end{lemma}

848	  \label{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
849	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_obj}
850	  \mathlibok

865	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
866	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
867	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
868	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
869	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
870	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
871	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
872	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
873	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
874	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
875	  the sheafification adjunction
876	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
877	  presheaves of modules, and write
878	  \[
879	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
880	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
881	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
882	  \]
883	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
884	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
885	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
886	  \[
887	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
888	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
889	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
890	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
891	  \]
892	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
893	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
894	\end{lemma}
895	
896	\begin{proof}
897	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
898	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
899	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
900	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
901	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
902	    lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
903	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
904	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
905	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
906	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
907	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
908	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
909	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
910	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
911	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
912	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
913	
914	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
915	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
916	  is the objectwise relative tensor
917	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
918	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
919	  maps
920	  \[
921	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
922	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
923	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
924	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
925	  \]
926	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
927	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
928	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
929	  pair
930	  \[
931	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
932	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
933	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
934	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
935	  \]
936	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
937	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
938	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
939	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
940	
941	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
942	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
943	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
944	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
945	  of \(a\)-images. It remains to see that the two whiskered comparison maps
946	  relating the rows,
947	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) and
948	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
949	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), lie in \(J.W\) (equivalently, become
950	  isomorphisms after applying \(a\)).
951	
952	  \textbf{Whiskered units are stalkwise isomorphisms.} We argue this via stalks
953	  of abelian-group presheaves (\(\mathrm{TopCat.Presheaf.stalk}\), which exist
954	  for abelian presheaves on the underlying space, unlike module-sheaf stalks).
955	  The sheafification unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to a\mathcal{P}\)
956	  is a \emph{stalkwise isomorphism}: for each point \(x\) the stalk map
957	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})_x : \mathcal{P}_x \to (a\mathcal{P})_x\) is an
958	  isomorphism (the defining property of the associated-sheaf unit). The stalk of
959	  a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of stalks,
960	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{B})_x \cong
961	  \mathcal{A}_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{B}_x\), because the stalk is a
962	  filtered colimit and \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) commutes with filtered colimits.
963	  Under this identification the stalk of
964	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) at \(x\) is
965	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), an isomorphism
966	  (tensoring an isomorphism with the identity). Hence each whiskered unit is a
967	  stalkwise isomorphism, and a stalkwise isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves
968	  on a topological space is a local isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\)
969	  (\cref{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}). Applying \(a\) therefore turns
970	  both whiskered comparison maps into isomorphisms.
971	
972	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
973	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
974	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
975	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
976	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
977	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
978	\end{proof}
979	
980	\begin{corollary}[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
981	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
982	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}
983	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
984	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
985	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
986	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
987	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
988	  \[
989	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
990	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
991	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
992	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
993	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
994	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
995	  \]
996	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
997	  arguments.
998	\end{corollary}
999	
1000	\begin{proof}
1001	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
1002	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1003	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1004	  Write \(a, b, c\) for the underlying presheaves of modules of
1005	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) and \((-)^{\#}\) for sheafification
1006	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
1007	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to
1008	  \[
1009	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
1010	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
1011	    \qquad
1012	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
1013	      = \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#},
1014	  \]
1015	  each carrying an \emph{inner} sheafification that obstructs a direct appeal to
1016	  the presheaf associator. The whiskered units remove these inner
1017	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
1018	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
1019	  \[
1020	    (\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#} :
1021	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
1022	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1023	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
1024	    = (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
